questions.text: ___________ is any combination of text, graphic art, sound, animation, and video delivered to you by computer or other electronic means.
questions.explanation: Multimedia is defined as the integration of various forms of media such as text, audio, images, animations, and video into a single digital presentation.

questions.text: ___________ multimedia allows an end user to control what and when the elements are delivered.
questions.explanation: Interactive multimedia gives the user the ability to control the flow and timing of the content being presented.

questions.text: ___________ is a structure of linked elements through which a user can navigate.
questions.explanation: Hypermedia is an extension of hypertext that includes links to other media forms like graphics, sound, and video, providing a navigational structure.

questions.text: A ___________ multimedia project allows users to sit back and watch it just as they watch a movie or the television.
questions.explanation: Linear multimedia projects play from beginning to end without user interaction, much like a traditional movie.

questions.text: ___________ tools are software tools designed to manage individual multimedia elements and provide user interaction.
questions.explanation: Authoring tools are used to assemble various media elements (text, audio, images) into a cohesive multimedia project with interactive features.

questions.text: The sum of what gets played back and how it is presented to the viewer on a monitor is the ___________.
questions.explanation: The Graphical User Interface (GUI) encompasses the visual presentation and the interactive elements that the user sees on the screen.

questions.text: The hardware and software that govern the limits of what can happen are the multimedia ___________.
questions.explanation: A platform refers to the combination of hardware and software (like an OS) that provides the environment in which multimedia software runs.

questions.text: The information that makes up a multimedia presentation is referred to as ___________.
questions.explanation: Content refers to the actual information and media (text, images, video) contained within the multimedia project.

questions.text: CD and DVD ___________ are used for reading and making discs.
questions.explanation: Burners (optical drives) are hardware devices capable of reading data from and writing data to CD and DVD media.

questions.text: HTML and DHTML Web pages or sites are generally viewed using a ___________.
questions.explanation: A web browser is the software application used to access and display information on the World Wide Web, interpreting HTML and DHTML code.

questions.text: LAN stands for:
questions.explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network, which is a network that connects computers and devices in a limited geographical area such as a home, school, or office building.

questions.text: A browser is used to view:
questions.explanation: Browsers are designed to render HTML and other web technologies to display web pages and online documents to users.

questions.text: The "ROM" in "CD-ROM" stands for:
questions.explanation: CD-ROM stands for Compact Disc Read-Only Memory, meaning the data on the disc is permanent and cannot be modified once manufactured (in standard ROM format).

questions.text: The software vehicle, messages, and content presented on a computer or television screen together make up a(n):
questions.explanation: A multimedia project is the collective term for the combined software, content, and interactive elements presented to the user.

questions.text: A project that is shipped or sold to consumers or end users, typically in a box or sleeve or on the Internet, with or without instructions, is a(n):
questions.explanation: When a multimedia project is packaged and distributed for sale or use by consumers, it is referred to as a multimedia title.

questions.text: The 19th-century Russian composer, who used an orchestra, a piano, a chorus, and a special color organ to synthesize music and color in his Fifth Symphony, *Prometheus*, was:
questions.explanation: Alexander Scriabin was a Russian composer known for his experiments with synesthesia and the creation of a 'clavier à lumières' (color organ) for his work *Prometheus: The Poem of Fire*.

questions.text: Which one of the following is/are NOT typically part of a multimedia specification?
questions.explanation: Standard digital multimedia involves text, sound, video, and graphics. Odors are not part of standard digital multimedia specifications.

questions.text: VR stands for:
questions.explanation: VR stands for Virtual Reality, a computer-generated environment with scenes and objects that appear to be real, making the user feel they are immersed in their surroundings.

questions.text: According to research in interactive multimedia presentations where you are really involved, the retention rate is as high as:
questions.explanation: According to educational research (often cited in Tay Vaughan's work), people retain about 20% of what they see and 30% of what they hear, but retention can reach 80% when they are actively 'doing' or involved in interactive multimedia.

questions.text: Which of the following is displayable on a Web page after the installation of a browser plug-in?
questions.explanation: Browser plug-ins like Macromedia Flash (now Adobe Flash) were historically used to enable the display of interactive multimedia content within a web browser.

questions.text: PDA stands for:
questions.explanation: PDA stands for Personal Data Assistant, a term for early handheld mobile devices used for organization and lightweight data management.

questions.text: The glass fiber cables that make up much of the physical backbone of the data highway are, in many cases, owned by:
questions.explanation: Historically, railroad and pipeline companies owned the right-of-ways across the country, allowing them to lay extensive fiber-optic networks used for the internet backbone.

questions.text: DVD stands for:
questions.explanation: While often called 'Digital Video Disc', the official technical term for DVD is Digital Versatile Disc, reflecting its ability to store data, audio, and video.

questions.text: At one time, the technology that brought the greatest amount of multimedia to the classroom was the:
questions.explanation: In the 1980s and early 90s, Laserdiscs were the primary high-capacity interactive multimedia technology used in educational environments before the widespread adoption of CD-ROMs.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a technology likely to prevail as a delivery means for interactive multimedia files?
questions.explanation: Floppy diskettes have very low storage capacity (typically 1.44MB), making them unsuitable for the large file sizes required for high-quality interactive multimedia.

questions.text: Macintosh is an operating system developed by __________.
questions.explanation: Macintosh (Mac OS) was developed by Apple Computer, Inc. (now Apple Inc.).

questions.text: Microsoft __________ comes in many versions, including 95, 98, ME, 2000, and XP.
questions.explanation: The versions listed (95, 98, ME, 2000, XP) are all iterations of the Microsoft Windows operating system.

questions.text: __________ is that stage of a project when the programs are tested to make sure that they meet the objectives of the project.
questions.explanation: The testing stage is dedicated to evaluating the software to ensure it functions correctly and fulfills project requirements.

questions.text: A __________ is a simple, working example that demonstrates whether or not an idea is feasible.
questions.explanation: A prototype (or proof-of-concept) is an early model created to test a concept or process.

questions.text: A __________ file requires no cross-platform conversion.
questions.explanation: Binary compatible files are designed to run or be read across different platforms without needing modification or translation.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a stage of a multimedia project?
questions.explanation: Marketing is generally considered a business function outside the core development stages (Planning, Design, Production, Testing, Delivery) of a multimedia project.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a common platform for producing and delivering multimedia projects?
questions.explanation: IBM VMS is not a standard or common platform for consumer-level multimedia production and delivery compared to Mac and Windows.

questions.text: Which hardware platform is considered by many multimedia developers to be better equipped to manage both sound and video editing?
questions.explanation: Apple computers have historically been favored in the creative industries for their robust handling of audio and video editing.

questions.text: The most precious asset that you can bring to a multimedia workshop is your:
questions.explanation: While technical skills are important, creativity is the primary driver for successful and innovative multimedia content.

questions.text: According to one quotation, "multimedia is creating, essentially, an entirely new syntax for":
questions.explanation: Multimedia provides a multifaceted way to convey information, effectively creating a new syntax for human communication.

questions.text: If, when trying to connect to a URL, you receive a "404 - unable to find" error message, you should:

choices.label: A
choices.text: contact the Web host to notify them of a core meltdown

choices.label: B
choices.text: try the same URL with a(n) ftp:// or smtp:// prefix

choices.label: C
choices.text: strip away directories and subdirectories and file names from the URL and connect to the domain name itself

choices.label: D
choices.text: restart the computer and try again

choices.label: E
choices.text: update your browser to a newer, more compatible version

questions.explanation: Stripping the URL back to the domain name helps determine if the server is up and if the error is due to an incorrect or moved specific file/path.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a danger to multimedia production?
questions.explanation: Breaking established rules is often a part of the creative process in multimedia and isn't inherently a threat to the production process like budget or compatibility issues.

questions.text: For multimedia production, mastering your medium means knowing:
questions.explanation: Understanding the technical capabilities and constraints of hardware and software is essential for 'mastering' the multimedia medium.

questions.text: The diverse range of abilities needed to produce a new-media project is called the __________.
questions.explanation: A multimedia skill set encompasses the various creative and technical talents required for production.

questions.text: The person responsible for the overall development and implementation of a project as well as for the day-to-day operations is the __________.
questions.explanation: The Project Manager handles both the high-level goals and the daily logistical tasks of the project.

questions.text: The most appropriate title for the person whose job it is to look at the overall content of a project, create a structure, determine the design elements, and assign media to the content is the __________.
questions.explanation: The Multimedia Designer is responsible for the look, feel, and organizational structure of the content.

questions.text: The work of a(n) __________ is best when it is "transparent" — never noticed by the user.
questions.explanation: Good interface design is intuitive; if it is 'transparent', the user navigates effortlessly without being conscious of the interface itself.

questions.text: The most appropriate title for the person whose job it is to devise the navigation pathways and content maps is the __________.
questions.explanation: Information Architects specialize in the organization of information and navigation structures within a project.

questions.text: The most appropriate title for the person whose job it is to structure content, determine user pathways and feedback, and select presentation media based on an awareness of the strengths of the many separate media that make up multimedia is the __________.
questions.explanation: Instructional Designers focus on pedagogy and the optimal way to structure media content for learning or communication.

questions.text: The job of a __________ is to create character, action, point of view, and interactivity, write proposals, script voice-overs and actors' narrations, and text screens.
questions.explanation: The script writer creates the narrative, dialogue, and textual elements that drive the interactive experience.

questions.text: The most appropriate title for the person whose job it is to integrate all the multimedia elements of a project into a seamless whole using an authoring system or programming language is the __________.
questions.explanation: The Multimedia Programmer (or Author) uses technical tools to assemble individual media components into the final product.

questions.text: Activities that help a group and its members function at optimum levels of performance by creating a work culture incorporating the styles of its members are called __________.
questions.explanation: Team building activities are designed to improve interpersonal relations and efficiency within a group.

questions.text: According to a recent study of occupations, which of these professions is NOT among the most respected in the United States?
questions.explanation: Surveys on occupational prestige often show that while lawyers are highly paid, they do not always rank as highly in 'prestige' or 'respect' as medical or scientific professionals.

questions.text: At Microsoft Corporation, the product manager:

choices.label: B
choices.text: represents the product to the outside world.

choices.label: D
choices.text: acquires the assets used in the project.

choices.label: E
choices.text: ensures that the project does not go over budget.

questions.explanation: In many large software organizations like Microsoft, the product manager's role is outward-facing, focusing on how the product is marketed and perceived by the public, while the program manager often handles internal coordination.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT likely to be the responsibility of a project manager?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Manage the overall development and implementation of a project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oversee budgets, schedules, creative sessions, and team dynamics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Act as the "glue" that holds the project together.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Understand the strengths and limitations of hardware and software.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Develop extensions to the authoring system.

questions.explanation: Developing extensions to an authoring system is a technical task performed by a multimedia programmer or software engineer, not the project manager.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT likely to be the responsibility of a multimedia designer?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Create budgets and timelines for the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure the visual consistency of the project.

questions.explanation: Creating budgets and timelines is the primary responsibility of the project manager, whereas the designer focuses on the structure, look, and feel of the content.

questions.text: According to one quote, the multimedia designer is sometimes called a(n):
questions.explanation: Multimedia designers are often referred to as information designers because their primary task is to organize and present information in an accessible and navigable way.

questions.text: According to one quote, from an interactive standpoint, many multimedia projects are too:
questions.explanation: A common criticism of multimedia projects is that they can be too passive, failing to engage the user with meaningful interactivity beyond clicking 'Next' buttons.

questions.text: An interface should:

choices.label: B
choices.text: provide control to the people who use it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: allow the user to move about within the project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide access to the "media" in the project.

questions.explanation: A good interface should be intuitive (transparent), empower the user with control, facilitate navigation, and allow easy access to the content.

questions.text: According to one quote, from a visual perspective, interface design most closely parallels:
questions.explanation: Interface design in multimedia is often compared to cinematography and film editing because it involves controlling the flow of information and the user's focus over time.

questions.text: According to one quote, interface designers should:

choices.label: D
choices.text: know how to do user testing.

questions.explanation: Interface designers need a diverse set of skills ranging from visual arts and media production to technical implementation and usability testing.

questions.text: Multimedia writers are typically involved in writing all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Authoring-language scripts are snippets of code used for logic and interactivity, which are typically handled by programmers rather than creative writers.

questions.text: Writing for multimedia can be more difficult than writing for other media because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: character development is much more critical.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the dramatic structures of multimedia are much more confined.

choices.label: C
choices.text: multimedia development cycles are much shorter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: writers must think in smaller, more discrete and interconnected units.

questions.explanation: The non-linear nature of multimedia requires writers to break content into discrete chunks (nodes) that can be navigated in various sequences, which is more complex than linear writing.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a necessary capability for a multimedia video specialist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Skills in managing all phases of video production.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Familiarity with the tools and techniques used for digital video editing on computers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ability to incorporate all the sophisticated video effects into a multimedia production.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ability to make a video look larger than it really is.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Familiarity with interactivity and how it will affect the video.

questions.explanation: While special effects are useful, having the ability to incorporate *all* sophisticated effects is not a 'necessary' requirement for the core role of a video specialist in a multimedia team.

questions.text: Which of the following is probably NOT a consideration of a multimedia audio specialist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sampling rate at which the audio will be delivered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: How much space is available for all audio?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Which authoring system or programming language to use.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Locating and selecting suitable music and talent.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Digitizing and editing recorded material into computer files.

questions.explanation: The choice of an authoring system or programming language is typically a high-level technical or project management decision, not an audio-specific task.

questions.text: According to one quote, the most important skill a multimedia programmer can bring to a team is the ability to:

choices.label: B
choices.text: control peripheral devices such as laserdisc players.

choices.label: C
choices.text: manage complex timing, transitions, and record keeping.

choices.label: D
choices.text: coax extra performance from multimedia authoring and programming systems.

choices.label: E
choices.text: act as a teacher and technical coach to the team.

questions.explanation: Because technology changes rapidly, the ability to adapt and learn new systems quickly is often cited as the most valuable trait for a programmer.

questions.text: Producing multimedia for the Web is different from producing for CD-ROM because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the Web development industry is much more focused on sales and marketing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Web development is much better adapted to larger, longer media.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Web design is much closer to print design; multimedia is more like film.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Web interface design is much more complex than CD-ROM interface design.

choices.label: E
choices.text: a Web site is never finished, but is always available for changes.

questions.explanation: Web content is dynamic and can be updated continuously, whereas a CD-ROM is a final physical product that cannot be changed once manufactured.

questions.text: Type sizes are usually expressed in:
questions.explanation: In typography, points (pt) are the standard unit of measurement for font height.

questions.text: When a password must be entered in exact upper or lower case to match the original, it is said to be:
questions.explanation: Case-sensitivity refers to a system's ability to distinguish between uppercase and lowercase characters.

questions.text: Symbolic representations of objects and processes common to graphical user interfaces (GUIs) are called:
questions.explanation: Icons are small visual symbols used in GUIs to represent files, folders, applications, or actions.

questions.text: Special HTML characters, always prefixed by an ampersand (&) and followed by a semicolon (;), are called:
questions.explanation: Character entities are used in HTML to display reserved characters or characters not present on a standard keyboard.

questions.text: The acronym WYSIWYG stands for:

choices.label: A
choices.text: What You Select Is What You Get

choices.label: B
choices.text: Where You See Is Where You Go

choices.label: C
choices.text: What You See Is What You Get

choices.label: D
choices.text: What You Store Is What You Get

questions.explanation: WYSIWYG describes a system where the content being edited looks exactly like the final output.

questions.text: Translating or designing multimedia into a language other than the original is called:
questions.explanation: Localization (l10n) involves adapting a product for a specific locale, including language translation and cultural adjustments.

questions.text: The little decoration at the end of a letter stroke is a:
questions.explanation: A serif is the small decorative line or flourish added to the ends of letter strokes in certain typefaces.

questions.text: Designers call roomy blank areas on a page or screen:
questions.explanation: White space (or negative space) refers to the unmarked areas between design elements.

questions.text: Which technique blends colors along the edges of letters to create a soft transition between the letter and its background?
questions.explanation: Anti-aliasing smooths jagged edges by blending the color of the edge pixels with the background color.

questions.text: Conceptual elements containing text, graphics, sounds, or related information in a knowledge base are called:
questions.explanation: In hypermedia systems, a node is a single unit or conceptual element of information.

questions.text: A family of graphic characters that usually includes many type sizes and styles is called a:
questions.explanation: A typeface is the design of a set of characters. A font refers to a specific weight and size of that typeface (e.g., Arial is a typeface; 12pt Arial Bold is a font).

questions.text: Which of the following is a term that applies to the spacing between character pairs?
questions.explanation: Kerning is the process of adjusting the space between specific pairs of characters to improve visual balance.

questions.text: Intercap, the practice of placing a capital in the middle of a word, is a trend that emerged from the computer programming community:

choices.label: B
choices.text: to copy marketing practices in the electronics industry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: to better see the words used by programmers for variables and commands.

choices.label: D
choices.text: because one of the first computer programmers had a faulty Shift key.

choices.label: E
choices.text: to increase security in case sensitive passwords.

questions.explanation: Intercap, also known as CamelCase, makes it easier for programmers to distinguish individual words within long variable or command names, as spaces are not allowed.

questions.text: Dynamic HTML uses _______________ to define choices ranging from line height to margin width to font face.
questions.explanation: Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) is the standard used in DHTML and modern web development to control the layout, formatting, and appearance of web documents.

questions.text: If a DHTML document includes a font face that is not installed on the user's computer, a browser will:

choices.label: C
choices.text: leave a blank space where that text is.

choices.label: E
choices.text: try to substitute the font with a similar font.

questions.explanation: When a specified font is unavailable, browsers default to a generic font family (like serif or sans-serif) or attempt to match it with a locally available alternative.

questions.text: In the URL `http://www.timestream.com/info/people/biotay/biotay1.html`, which part is case sensitive?
questions.explanation: While protocols and domain names are case-insensitive, the document path (folders and filenames) on a web server (especially Unix/Linux-based) is typically case-sensitive.

questions.text: Multimedia becomes interactive multimedia when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the user has some control over what information is viewed and when it is viewed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the information is displayed by a computer with a touchscreen or other input device.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the information is available on the Web, either the Internet or a Local Area Network.

choices.label: D
choices.text: quizzes and tests with evaluations and scoring are included.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the user can change such attributes as volume and type size.

questions.explanation: Interactivity is defined by the user's ability to engage with the content and influence the flow or delivery of the information.

questions.text: Interactive multimedia becomes hypermedia when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the information is available on the Web, either the Internet or a Local Area Network.

choices.label: B
choices.text: quizzes and tests with evaluations and scoring are included.

choices.label: C
choices.text: it includes a structure of linked elements through which a user can navigate and interact.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the user can change such attributes as volume and type size.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the content formatting complies with the American Standard Code for Information Interchange.

questions.explanation: Hypermedia is the extension of hypertext to include other media (video, audio, etc.) organized in a non-linear network of nodes and links.

questions.text: Web pages are coded using:

choices.label: B
choices.text: American Standard Code for Information Interchange.

questions.explanation: HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) is the fundamental markup language used to create and structure pages on the World Wide Web.

questions.text: Which of the following provides a system for dynamically displaying a font?
questions.explanation: PostScript is a page description language used in the electronic publishing and desktop publishing realms to describe the appearance of a printed page, including vector fonts.

questions.text: A printed page might be presented in which of these orientations?
questions.explanation: Portrait and Landscape are the two primary orientations for printed pages, with Portrait being taller than it is wide.

questions.text: Which of the following is a character encoding system?
questions.explanation: Unicode is an international standard for consistent encoding, representation, and handling of text expressed in most of the world's writing systems.

questions.text: The reference from one document to another document, image, sound, or file on the Web is a(n):
questions.explanation: In HTML, the anchor tag (\(<a>\)) is used to define a hyperlink, which is a reference that allows users to move from one location to another.

questions.text: What is a general measurement applied to individual characters called?
questions.explanation: Character metrics refer to the specific physical dimensions and properties of characters in a font, such as width, height, and spacing.

questions.text: Which of the following is a typical method for word searching in a hypermedia system?
questions.explanation: Adjacency searching allows users to find documents where specific words appear close to one another, which is a common feature in sophisticated search engines.

questions.text: The branch of physics that studies sound is known as:
questions.explanation: Acoustics is the interdisciplinary science that deals with the study of all mechanical waves in gases, liquids, and solids including topics such as vibration, sound, ultrasound, and infrasound.

questions.text: Sound pressure levels, which correspond to loudness or volume, are measured in:
questions.explanation: Decibels are a logarithmic unit used to express the ratio of two values of a physical quantity, often power or intensity, commonly used to measure sound levels.

questions.text: The process of adjusting the level of multiple audio tracks to bring them to a consistent volume level is called:
questions.explanation: Normalization is the process of applying a constant amount of gain to an audio recording to bring the peak power or average power to a target level.

questions.text: When audio is digitized, the process of rounding each measurement value to the nearest integer is called:
questions.explanation: Quantization in digital signal processing is the process of mapping continuous infinite values to a smaller set of discrete finite values, often involving rounding.

questions.text: The file format that uses a shorthand representation of musical notes and durations stored in numeric form is:
questions.explanation: MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) does not record actual audio waveforms; instead, it stores digital data (commands) like note on/off, pitch, and velocity.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic or advantage of MIDI files?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sound can easily be changed by changing instruments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spoken audio can easily be included.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sound tracks can be created using sequencing software.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Files are generally smaller than the same digital audio sound.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sounds can be stretched and timing changed with no distortion of the quality.

questions.explanation: MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) files contain instructional data (which notes to play and when) rather than actual recorded audio. Therefore, they cannot store complex wave patterns like spoken dialogue, which requires digital audio recording.

questions.text: The primary benefit of the General MIDI over the previous MIDI specification is that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the file sizes are much smaller due to the compression scheme

choices.label: B
choices.text: users can easily edit and adjust the data structures

choices.label: C
choices.text: it can be easily converted into the CD-ROM/XA format

choices.label: D
choices.text: MIDI files can be easily integrated into the computer's operating system as system sounds.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the instruments are the same regardless of the playback source.

questions.explanation: General MIDI (GM) was established to standardize instrument patch mappings. Prior to GM, a MIDI file might play as a piano on one synthesizer and a drum kit on another. GM ensures that 'Program 1' is always an Acoustic Grand Piano across all compliant devices.

questions.text: What happens when an audio signal exceeds the recording device's maximum recording level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The signal is compressed to an appropriate level.

choices.label: B
choices.text: "Clipping" of the signal occurs, introducing distortion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The audio clip is extended to accommodate the extra data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The entire clip's volume is reduced correspondingly.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The extra bits go into a buffer for later use.

questions.explanation: When a signal is too loud for the hardware to handle, the tops and bottoms of the waveforms are cut off (clipped), which creates harsh harmonic distortion.

questions.text: As one story goes, the criterion used to set the length of the sectors and ultimately the physical size of the compact disc format was based on the length of:
questions.explanation: Legend suggests that the CD was designed to hold approximately 74 minutes of audio specifically so that it could accommodate a full performance of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony without interruption.

questions.text: The process of recording a sound, stored in the form of thousands of individual measurements, each at a discrete point in time, is called:
questions.explanation: Sampling is the process of taking discrete 'snapshots' of an analog signal's amplitude at regular intervals to represent it digitally.

questions.text: The file size of a 5-second recording sampled at 22 kHz, 16-bit stereo (two tracks) would be about:
questions.explanation: Calculation: \( \text{Sample Rate (22,000)} \times \text{Sample Size (2 bytes for 16-bit)} \times \text{Channels (2 for stereo)} \times \text{Duration (5s)} = 440,000 \text{ bytes} \).

questions.text: Which of the following sound file characteristics does not directly affect the size of a digital audio file?
questions.explanation: Volume is the amplitude data stored within the samples, but changing the volume of a clip does not change the amount of data (bits) required to store it. Sample rate, bit depth (size), number of tracks, and compression all directly change the file footprint.

questions.text: Each individual measurement of a sound that is stored as digital information is called a:
questions.explanation: A 'sample' represents the amplitude of the audio signal at a specific point in time.

questions.text: Audio recorded at 44.1 kHz (kilohertz), 16-bit stereo is considered:
questions.explanation: The standard specification for Audio CDs (Red Book) is a sampling rate of 44,100 Hz and a bit depth of 16 bits.

questions.text: Removing blank space or "dead air" at the beginning or end of a recording is sometimes called:
questions.explanation: Trimming refers to cutting the start or end points of an audio file to remove unwanted silence or noise.

questions.text: DSP stands for:
questions.explanation: DSP (Digital Signal Processing) is the mathematical manipulation of an information signal, such as audio or video, to improve or modify it.

questions.text: Sequencing software:

choices.label: A
choices.text: places audio clips in order in a sound track

choices.label: C
choices.text: applies filters to digital audio clips in a predetermined order

choices.label: D
choices.text: manages a project by creating a timeline of events

choices.label: E
choices.text: helps synchronize images with a sound track

questions.explanation: A sequencer is a hardware or software tool used to record, edit, and play back MIDI performance data.

questions.text: The slower a user's connection, the longer he or she must wait for enough of the sound to download so that the entire file will have downloaded by the time the sound reaches the end. This effect is called:
questions.explanation: Latency in streaming refers to the delay between the request for data and the start of the data being received or played back, often caused by buffer requirements.

questions.text: The Red Book standard was so named because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the standard was pioneered in the former Soviet Union

choices.label: B
choices.text: red is an acronym for "Registered Electronic Data"

choices.label: C
choices.text: the standard's book jacket was red

choices.label: D
choices.text: it was so expensive to produce CDs early on that most producers were "in the red"

choices.label: E
choices.text: the dye in the first recordable CDs had a reddish tint

questions.explanation: Philips and Sony published a series of technical specifications for different CD formats, known as the 'Rainbow Books'. The Audio CD standard was physically bound in a red cover.

questions.text: The working area of a computer monitor is sometimes called the:
questions.explanation: In graphical user interfaces, the desktop is the primary area of the screen where windows, icons, and menus are displayed.

questions.text: The type of image used for photo-realistic images and for complex drawings requiring fine detail is the:
questions.explanation: Bitmaps (or raster images) store information for every individual pixel, making them ideal for complex, detailed images like photographs.

questions.text: The type of image used for lines, boxes, circles, polygons, and other graphic shapes that can be mathematically expressed in angles, coordinates, and distances is the:
questions.explanation: Vector graphics use mathematical formulas to draw shapes, allowing them to be scaled infinitely without losing quality.

questions.text: The picture elements that make up a bitmap are called:
questions.explanation: A pixel (short for 'picture element') is the smallest unit of a digital image that can be displayed or represented on a digital display device.

questions.text: Which process allows you to smoothly blend two images so that one image seems to melt into another?
questions.explanation: Morphing is a special effect in motion pictures and animations that changes (or morphs) one image or shape into another through a seamless transition.

questions.text: The process that computes the bounds of the shapes of colors within a bitmap image and then derives the polygon object that describes that image is called:
questions.explanation: Autotracing is the process of converting a bitmap (raster) image into a vector format by identifying paths and shapes.

questions.text: Which term describes when the computer finally uses intricate algorithms to apply the effects you have specified on the objects you have created for a final 3D image?
questions.explanation: Rendering is the final step in the 3D creation process where the computer generates a 2D image or animation from a 3D scene.

questions.text: Which of the following provides a method for viewing a single surrounding image as if you were "inside" the picture and able to look up or down, turn, or zoom in on features?
questions.explanation: QuickTime VR (Virtual Reality) is an older technology that allows for the creation and viewing of photorealistic panoramas and 360-degree object rotations.

questions.text: An annoying flash of strange colors in your image while the computer remakes its color lookup table and the old colors change to the new is called:
questions.explanation: Palette flashing (or palette shift) occurs when an application changes the system's color palette, causing other windows to display with incorrect, often garish, colors momentarily.

questions.text: The process whereby the color value of each pixel is changed to the closest matching color value in the target palette, using a mathematical algorithm, is called:
questions.explanation: Dithering is a technique used in computer graphics to create the illusion of color depth in images with a limited color palette by arranging pixels of available colors.

questions.text: The way(s) to start creating your project's interface is/are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: start with pencil, eraser, and paper.

choices.label: B
choices.text: outline your project and graphic ideas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: use 3 x 5 index cards and shuffle them.

questions.explanation: Initial design and brainstorming for a user interface often involve low-fidelity prototyping methods like sketching on paper, outlining, storyboarding, and using index cards to organize content flow.

questions.text: Which image file type is the best for photographs?
questions.explanation: Bitmaps (also known as raster images) are best for photographs because they store color information for every individual pixel, allowing for the complex color gradations found in real-world images.

questions.text: A 16-bit image is capable of representing how many different colors?
questions.explanation: The number of colors is determined by \(2^n\), where \(n\) is the bit depth. For a 16-bit image, \(2^{16} = 65,536\) possible colors.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a vector-drawn object?
questions.explanation: Photographs are raster (bitmap) images composed of pixels. Vector graphics are made of mathematical paths (lines, circles, polygons) and are resolution-independent.

questions.text: "Unlimited use" of stock photography may actually impose a limitation on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the number of units you can distribute without paying more.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the number of changes you can make to the image.

choices.label: C
choices.text: converting the image to another file format.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the filters you may use to alter the image.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the price you can charge for your product.

questions.explanation: Even with "unlimited use" licenses, stock photo agencies often have a cap on the number of physical or digital copies (e.g., 500,000 units) that can be distributed before an extended license is required.

questions.text: The area of memory where data such as text and images is temporarily stored when you cut or copy them within an application is called the:
questions.explanation: The clipboard is a software facility that is used for short-term data storage and transfer between documents or applications via copy and paste operations.

questions.text: Perhaps the single most significant advance in computer image processing during the late 1980s was the development of:
questions.explanation: The development of sophisticated image-editing software (like the precursors to Adobe Photoshop) in the late 1980s revolutionized how digital images were manipulated.

questions.text: When an image imported on a Macintosh is viewed on a PC, it appears:

choices.label: A
choices.text: darker and richer because the values have changed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: lighter and less saturated because the values have changed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: darker and richer even though the values have not changed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: lighter and less saturated even though the values have not changed.

questions.explanation: Historically, Macintosh systems used a gamma of 1.8 while PCs used 2.2. Because of this difference in display gamma, Mac images often look darker and more saturated on a PC screen, despite the raw data values remaining the same.

questions.text: Graphic artists designing for print media use vector-drawn objects because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: they can contain more subtle variations in shading than bitmap graphics.

choices.label: B
choices.text: printing inks respond better to them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: they can be converted across platforms more easily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: they can be printed in any size.

choices.label: E
choices.text: they can be viewed directly in Web browsers.

questions.explanation: Vector graphics are resolution-independent. They are based on mathematical formulas, allowing them to be scaled to any size (from a business card to a billboard) without losing quality or becoming pixelated.

questions.text: The 3D process of extending a plane surface some distance, either perpendicular to the shape's outline or along a defined path, is called:
questions.explanation: Extruding is a fundamental 3D modeling technique where a 2D shape is pushed into the third dimension to create volume.

questions.text: The VGA standard is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 8 bits of color information per pixel in a matrix of 640 pixels across and 480 pixels down.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 16 bits of color information per pixel in a matrix of 640 pixels across and 480 pixels down.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 16 bits of color information per pixel in a matrix of 800 pixels across and 600 pixels down.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 24 bits of color information per pixel in a matrix of 800 pixels across and 600 pixels down.

choices.label: E
choices.text: 24 bits of color information per pixel in a matrix of 1024 pixels across and 768 pixels down.

questions.explanation: Standard VGA resolution is 640x480. While the hardware supported different modes, the 256-color (8-bit) mode at 640x480 is a baseline standard for multimedia history.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct HTML representation of magenta (red + blue)?
questions.explanation: In HTML hexadecimal color codes, the format is #RRGGBB. Magenta is a combination of full Red (FF) and full Blue (FF) with no Green (00), resulting in #FF00FF.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a color specification format?
questions.explanation: RGB, HSB, CMYK, and CIE are all color models or specifications. GIF is a file format (Graphics Interchange Format).

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a native Windows graphics file format?
questions.explanation: PICT is the native graphics format for the original Macintosh operating system (QuickDraw). BMP, RIFF, and PCX are native to Windows/DOS environments.

questions.text: TIFF stands for:
questions.explanation: TIFF stands for Tagged Image File Format (sometimes also referred to as Tagged Interchange File Format in older texts).

questions.text: The working area of a computer monitor is sometimes referred to as:
questions.explanation: Screen 'real estate' is a common industry term referring to the amount of space available on a display for windows and data.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the picture elements that make up a bitmap?
questions.explanation: Bitmaps are composed of discrete dots called pixels (picture elements). In some technical contexts, they are also referred to as 'pels'.

questions.text: What is the term for smoothly blending two images so that one image seems to melt into another?
questions.explanation: Morphing is a special effect in motion pictures and animations that changes one image or shape into another through a seamless transition.

questions.text: The process that computes the bounds of the shapes of colors within a bitmap image and then derives the polygon object that describes that image is called:
questions.explanation: Autotracing software analyzes a bitmap and creates vector paths that match the edges of color regions.

questions.text: What is the term for an annoying flash of strange colors in your image while the computer remakes its color lookup table?
questions.explanation: Palette flashing occurs on 8-bit systems when an application changes the system color palette, causing other visible windows to briefly display incorrect colors.

questions.text: An object seen by the human eye remains chemically mapped on the eye’s retina for a brief time after viewing. This phenomenon is called:
questions.explanation: Persistence of vision refers to the optical illusion that occurs when visual perception of an object does not cease for some time after the rays of light proceeding from it have ceased to enter the eye.

questions.text: The human mind needs to conceptually complete a perceived action. This phenomenon is called:
questions.explanation: The phi phenomenon is the optical illusion of perceiving continuous motion between separate objects viewed rapidly in succession.

questions.text: To make an object travel across the screen while it changes its shape, you should change the shape and also:

choices.label: B
choices.text: reposition or move it a few pixels for each frame.

choices.label: D
choices.text: invert its colors for each frame.

questions.explanation: To simulate movement across the screen, the object's coordinates (position) must be updated in each successive frame.

questions.text: The animation technique made famous by Disney involves showing a different image for each frame. This technique is called:
questions.explanation: Traditional cel animation is the process of drawing each frame of animation on transparent sheets of celluloid.

questions.text: The first and last frames of an action are called:
questions.explanation: Keyframes define the start and end points of any smooth transition in animation.

questions.text: The series of frames between the first and last frames in an action are drawn in a process called:
questions.explanation: Tweening, short for in-betweening, is the process of generating intermediate frames between two images to give the appearance that the first image evolves smoothly into the second image.

questions.text: In computer animation terminology, what usually refers to special methods that allow images to blend or otherwise mix their colors to produce special transparencies, inversions, and effects?
questions.explanation: Ink effects are used to specify how the pixels of an object interact with the pixels of objects in layers behind it.

questions.text: The study of the movement and motion of structures that have joints is called:
questions.explanation: Kinematics is the study of motion without consideration of forces. In animation, it specifically refers to the motion of jointed structures.

questions.text: The effect in which one image transforms into another is known as:
questions.explanation: Morphing is a special effect in motion pictures and animations that changes (or morphs) one image or shape into another through a seamless transition.

questions.text: Most authoring packages include visual effects such as:
questions.explanation: Transition effects like wipes, fades, and dissolves are standard features in multimedia authoring tools.

questions.text: The term cel derives from:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the concept of each action in a sequence being a separate element or “cell.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: the fact that the inks used in early animations were based on extracts from celery plants.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an abbreviation of the phrase “composite element.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: the fact that the first animations were the work of communist dissidents who were organized into cells.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the clear celluloid sheets that were used for drawing each frame.

questions.explanation: A cel is a transparent sheet on which objects are drawn or painted for traditional, hand-drawn animation. It is short for celluloid.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a reason why animation is perceived as motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An image remains in the eye chemically for a brief time after viewing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Our mind tries to “connect the dots” by completing perceived actions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The use of darker colors for moving objects is interpreted by the mind as motion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A sequence of images is read as continuous motion.

choices.label: E
choices.text: All of the above are valid reasons.

questions.explanation: Color choice (darker or lighter) does not fundamentally determine the physiological or psychological perception of motion in a series of images.

questions.text: Movies on film are typically shot at a shutter rate of:
questions.explanation: The standard frame rate for cinematic film is 24 frames per second.

questions.text: The clear sheets that were used for drawing each frame of animation have been replaced today by:
questions.explanation: In traditional animation history, flammable celluloid was replaced by safety film made of cellulose acetate or similar plastics.

questions.text: Today’s computer animation programs most closely resemble:

choices.label: B
choices.text: the “phi” phenomenon described by Carl Jung.

questions.explanation: Modern digital animation software uses a layer-based system that directly mimics the stacking of traditional transparent cels.

questions.text: The file format that is designed to contain animation is:
questions.explanation: In the context of multimedia history, Macromedia Director's native file format was designed specifically to hold complex multimedia and animations.

questions.text: In general, animation may appear jerky and slow if each frame is displayed for more than about:
questions.explanation: A frame rate of at least 15 frames per second (1/15th of a second per frame) is generally required to maintain the illusion of smooth motion.

questions.text: The process in which you link objects such as hands to arms and define their relationships and limits (for example, elbows cannot bend backwards), then drag these parts around and let the computer calculate the result is called:
questions.explanation: Inverse kinematics (IK) is a mathematical process used in 3D animation to calculate the joint movements required to place an end effector (like a hand) in a specific position.

questions.text: To create a smooth transition between two images when morphing, it is important to set numerous:
questions.explanation: Key points (or control points) are used to align features between the source and target images to guide the deformation and color blending during a morph.

questions.text: The standard frame rate of computer animation is:

choices.label: E
choices.text: There is no standard; it depends on a file’s settings.

questions.explanation: In early multimedia textbooks, 15 frames per second was often cited as the common standard for computer-based multimedia animation to save processing power and file size.

questions.text: Today, the most widely used tool for creating multimedia animations is:
questions.explanation: Historically, Flash (developed by Macromedia and later acquired by Adobe) was the dominant tool for web-based and multimedia animations.

questions.text: What extension does the Director file format have?
questions.explanation: Macromedia Director used .dir for project files and .dcr for compressed, web-ready Shockwave files.

questions.text: The file format that is most widely supported for Web animations is:
questions.explanation: GIF89a was the first widely supported image format on the web that allowed for simple, frame-based animation.

questions.text: To keep the post-compression file size at absolute minimums, Flash makes extensive use of:
questions.explanation: Flash uses vector graphics because they describe shapes with mathematical formulas rather than pixels. This allows the software to achieve high-quality visuals with very small file sizes compared to bitmapped (raster) graphics.

questions.text: Which phenomenon describes the occurrence where an object seen by the human eye remains chemically mapped on the retina for a brief period after viewing?
questions.explanation: Persistence of vision is the optical illusion that allows the eye to retain an image for a fraction of a second after the image is gone, providing the basis for the perception of motion in cinema and animation.

questions.text: What is the phenomenon called when the human mind conceptually completes a perceived action?
questions.explanation: The phi phenomenon is the optical illusion of perceiving a series of still images as continuous motion because the brain fills in the gaps between images.

questions.text: To make an object travel across the screen while its shape changes, you must change the shape and also ______ it by a few pixels for each frame.
questions.explanation: Translation is the mathematical and geometric term for moving an object from one location to another without changing its orientation or size.

questions.text: Which animation technique, popularized by Disney, involves displaying a unique, hand-drawn image for every frame of the animation?
questions.explanation: Cel animation is the traditional animation method where characters and objects are drawn on transparent celluloid sheets and layered over static backgrounds.

questions.text: In the terminology of animation, the first and last frames that define a specific action are known as:
questions.explanation: Keyframes define the significant points of change in an animation, such as the start and end of a movement, providing the basis for interpolation.

questions.text: What is the process called for creating the series of frames that sit between the first and last frames of an action?
questions.explanation: Tweening, short for in-betweening, is the process of generating intermediate frames between two keyframes to give the appearance that the first image evolves smoothly into the second image.

questions.text: In computer animation, which term refers to special methods used to blend images or mix colors to create effects like transparency and inversion?
questions.explanation: In programs like Macromedia Director or Flash, 'inks' are used to define how sprite colors react with colors from objects in background layers or other sprites.

questions.text: The study of the movement and motion of structures specifically containing joints is known as:
questions.explanation: Kinematics focuses on describing the motion of objects, including points and jointed systems (like skeletons), without considering the forces that cause the motion.

questions.text: What is the term for the effect where one image seamlessly transforms into another?
questions.explanation: Morphing is a digital animation technique used to create a fluid transformation from one image to another through a sequence of intermediate shapes.

questions.text: What is defined as any combination of text, graphic art, sound, animation, and video delivered to you by computer or other electronic means?
questions.explanation: Multimedia is the integration of multiple forms of media including text, graphics, audio, video, and animation into a single digital presentation.

questions.text: Which type of multimedia allows an end user to control what and when the elements are delivered?
questions.explanation: Interactive multimedia provides the user with control over the sequence and timing of the content being presented.

questions.text: What is the term for a structure of linked elements through which a user can navigate?
questions.explanation: Hypermedia is an extension of hypertext that includes graphics, audio, video, and plain text as elements in a non-linear network of information.

questions.text: A project that allows users to sit back and watch it just as they watch a movie or the television is known as a ________ multimedia project.
questions.explanation: Linear multimedia projects progress without any navigational control by the viewer, much like a traditional film.

questions.text: Which type of software tools are designed to manage individual multimedia elements and provide user interaction?
questions.explanation: Authoring tools are used to assemble various media elements into a cohesive interactive project.

questions.text: The sum of what gets played back and how it is presented to the viewer on a monitor is the ________.
questions.explanation: The GUI defines the visual representation and the method of interaction between the user and the multimedia content.

questions.text: The hardware and software that govern the limits of what can happen are referred to as the multimedia ________.
questions.explanation: The multimedia platform consists of the hardware (e.g., PC, console) and OS that provide the environment for running multimedia applications.

questions.text: The information that makes up a multimedia presentation is referred to as:
questions.explanation: In multimedia, 'content' refers to the actual information being communicated (text, video, audio).

questions.text: Which devices are used for both reading and making (writing) CD and DVD discs?
questions.explanation: CD/DVD burners use lasers to write data onto discs and can also read them.

questions.text: HTML and DHTML Web pages or sites are generally viewed using a:
questions.explanation: Web browsers like Chrome, Firefox, or Safari are the standard applications used to interpret and display HTML/DHTML content.

questions.text: Type sizes are usually expressed in _________________________.
questions.explanation: Type sizes are measured in points, where 72 points approximately equal one inch.

questions.text: When a password is entered in upper or lower case to match the original password, it is said to be _________________________.
questions.explanation: Case sensitive means the system distinguishes between uppercase and lowercase characters.

questions.text: Symbolic representations of objects and processes common to the graphical user interfaces of many computer operating systems are called _________________________.
questions.explanation: Icons are small symbolic pictures used in GUIs to represent files, folders, or programs.

questions.text: Special HTML characters, always prefixed by an ampersand (escape) and followed by a semicolon, are called _________________________.
questions.explanation: Character entities (like `©`) are used in HTML to display reserved characters or symbols.

choices.label: A
choices.text: What You See Is What You Get

choices.label: B
choices.text: What You Say Is What You Get

choices.label: C
choices.text: Web Yields Simple Information With Your Graphics

choices.label: D
choices.text: When Your Software Is Working Yield Greatly

questions.explanation: WYSIWYG refers to a system where the on-screen content closely resembles the final output.

questions.text: Translating or designing multimedia (or any computer-based material) into a language other than the one in which it was originally written is called _________________________.
questions.explanation: Localization is the process of adapting content to a specific locale, including language translation.

questions.text: The little decoration at the end of a letter stroke is a _________________________.
questions.explanation: A serif is a small decorative line added as a stroke to the end of a letter's main shape.

questions.text: Designers call roomy blank areas _________________________.
questions.explanation: White space (or negative space) refers to areas in a design that are left empty.

questions.text: _________________________ blends the colors along the edges of the letters to create a soft transition between the letter and its background.
questions.explanation: Anti-aliasing smooths jagged edges by adding intermediate colored pixels.

questions.text: Conceptual elements, containing text, graphics, sounds, or related information in the knowledge base, are called _________________________.
questions.explanation: In hypermedia systems, nodes are the discrete items of information connected by links.

questions.text: Dynamic HTML uses _________________________ to define choices ranging from line height to margin width to font face.
questions.explanation: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is used in DHTML to control the presentation and layout of web documents.

questions.text: Interactive multimedia becomes hypermedia when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: it is available on the Internet or a Local Area Network.

choices.label: C
choices.text: it includes a structure of linked elements through which a user can navigate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the content formatting complies with ASCII.

questions.explanation: Hypermedia is defined by the use of links to navigate between different pieces of multimedia content.

questions.text: Web pages are coded using:
questions.explanation: HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) is the standard markup language for creating web pages.

questions.text: The branch of physics that studies sound is:
questions.explanation: Acoustics is the multidisciplinary science that deals with the study of all mechanical waves in gases, liquids, and solids including topics such as vibration, sound, ultrasound, and infrasound.

questions.text: Sound pressure levels (loudness or volume) are measured in:
questions.explanation: Sound intensity and pressure levels are measured in decibels (dB).

questions.text: The process of adjusting the level of a number of tracks to bring them all up to about the same level is called:
questions.explanation: Normalization is the process of increasing or decreasing the amplitude of an entire audio signal so that the peak reaches a specific target level.

questions.text: When audio is measured to be digitally stored, the value of each measurement is rounded off to the nearest integer in a process called:
questions.explanation: Quantization in digital signal processing is the process of mapping continuous infinite values to a smaller set of discrete finite values, essentially rounding signal amplitudes to the nearest bit depth level.

questions.text: Reducing the number of separate measurements of an audio file is called:
questions.explanation: Downsampling (or decimation) reduces the sampling rate of a signal, effectively reducing the number of measurements taken per second.

questions.text: The standard file format for displaying digitized motion video on the Macintosh is:
questions.explanation: QuickTime is the native multimedia framework developed by Apple for the Macintosh operating system.

questions.text: The most common file format for editing sound on the Macintosh is:
questions.explanation: AIFF (Audio Interchange File Format) is the standard uncompressed audio format used natively on Macintosh systems.

questions.text: The audio file format introduced by Microsoft and IBM with the introduction of Windows is the:
questions.explanation: WAV (Waveform Audio File Format) was developed by Microsoft and IBM as the standard for storing audio bitstreams on Windows PCs.

questions.text: The process of playing a sound file while part of the file is still downloading is called:
questions.explanation: Streaming allows media to be consumed as it is being delivered from a provider over the internet.

questions.text: Some software allows you to begin playing a downloading sound file as soon as enough of the sound is cached in your computer's:
questions.explanation: Data being downloaded is usually buffered or cached in the system's random-access memory (RAM) for immediate playback.

questions.text: Which of these statements regarding the MIDI audio format is NOT true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sound can easily be changed by changing instruments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spoken audio can easily be included.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sound tracks can be created using sequencing software.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Files are generally smaller than the same digital audio sound.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sounds can be stretched and timing changed with no distortion of the quality.

questions.explanation: MIDI is a protocol for musical instructions and does not record raw sound. Therefore, complex waveforms like spoken voice cannot be represented as MIDI data.

questions.text: Audio recorded at \( 44.1 \text{ kHz} \) (kilohertz), 16-bit stereo is considered:
questions.explanation: The standard for CD-quality audio is a sampling rate of \( 44.1 \text{ kHz} \) with a 16-bit depth in stereo.

questions.text: Sequencing software is primarily used to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: place audio clips in order in a sound track

choices.label: C
choices.text: apply filters to digital audio clips

choices.label: D
choices.text: help synchronize images with a sound track

questions.explanation: A sequencer is a piece of software (or hardware) used to record, edit, and play back MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) data.

questions.text: The slower a user's connection, the longer they must wait for enough of the sound to download so that the file will have downloaded by the time the sound reaches the end. This effect is called:
questions.explanation: Streaming latency is the delay caused by waiting for a buffer to fill before playback begins to ensure smooth performance.

questions.text: The Red Book standard for audio CDs was so named because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: red is an acronym for "Registered Electronic Data"

choices.label: B
choices.text: it was so expensive to produce CDs early on

choices.label: C
choices.text: the standard's book jacket was red

choices.label: D
choices.text: the dye in early CDs had a reddish tint

questions.explanation: The 'Rainbow Books' are a collection of standards for CD formats; the audio CD standard (CD-DA) was published in a red binder.

questions.text: An object seen by the human eye remains chemically mapped on the eye’s retina for a brief time after viewing. This phenomenon is called:
questions.explanation: Persistence of vision is the optical illusion that occurs when visual perception of an object does not cease for some time after the rays of light proceeding from it have ceased to enter the eye.

questions.text: The phenomenon where the human mind conceptually completes a perceived action to create the illusion of motion is called:
questions.explanation: The phi phenomenon is the optical illusion of perceiving continuous motion between separate objects viewed rapidly in succession.

questions.text: To make an object travel across the screen while it changes its shape, one must change the shape and also move or ________ it a few pixels for each frame.
questions.explanation: Moving or repositioning an object slightly in each subsequent frame creates the visual illusion of translation (movement) across a space.

questions.text: The animation technique involving showing a different hand-drawn image for each frame is called:
questions.explanation: Cel animation is the traditional animation technique where each frame is drawn by hand on transparent celluloid sheets.

questions.text: The first and last frames of an animation sequence are called:
questions.explanation: Keyframes are the specific frames in a sequence that define the starting and ending points of any smooth transition or movement.

questions.text: The process of generating the series of frames between the first and last frames in an action is called:
questions.explanation: Tweening, short for in-betweening, is the process of generating intermediate frames between two keyframes to create the appearance of motion.

questions.text: In computer animation, ________ refer to special methods that allow images to blend colors to produce transparencies, inversions, and other effects.
questions.explanation: In early animation and authoring software like Macromedia Director, 'Inks' were the settings used to define how an object's pixels blended with the background.

questions.text: The study of the movement and motion of structures that have joints is called:
questions.explanation: Kinematics is the branch of mechanics concerned with the motion of objects without reference to the forces which cause the motion.

questions.text: The term "cel" in animation derives from:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the concept of an action sequence being a separate "cell"

choices.label: C
choices.text: the clear celluloid sheets that were used for drawing

questions.explanation: Traditional animation 'cels' are named after the celluloid (cellulose nitrate) sheets used as a transparent medium for hand-drawn frames.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a physiological or psychological reason why animation is perceived as motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An image remains in the eye chemically for a brief time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mind completes perceived actions by "connecting the dots."

choices.label: C
choices.text: Darker colors for moving objects are interpreted as motion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A sequence of images is read as continuous motion.

questions.explanation: Color brightness or darkness is not a fundamental principle of motion perception; persistence of vision and the phi phenomenon are the core concepts.

questions.text: The clear sheets originally used for drawing each frame of animation have been largely replaced today by:
questions.explanation: Celluloid was replaced by more stable, non-flammable acetate or plastic sheets before digital animation became the dominant industry standard.

questions.text: A hard disk system that will support high-speed data transfer rates is called a:
questions.explanation: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) can be configured to stripe data across multiple disks to significantly increase data read and write speeds.

questions.text: The television signal format used in the United States, Japan, and many other countries is known as:
questions.explanation: NTSC (National Television System Committee) is the analog television standard historically used in North America and parts of Asia and South America.

questions.text: Television screens use a process of building a single frame from two fields to help prevent flicker on television screens in a technique called:
questions.explanation: Interlacing splits a single frame into two separate fields consisting of odd and even lines, which are displayed in rapid succession to provide smoother motion with less flicker on analog displays.

questions.text: When creating graphics for conversion to video, do not place any critical information such as text in the outside 15 percent of the image. Instead, keep it within the:
questions.explanation: The safe title area is the inner region of a video frame where text is guaranteed not to be cut off by the edges of older CRT television sets.

questions.text: The technique in which playback of a video starts as soon as enough data has transferred to the user’s computer to sustain this playback is called:
questions.explanation: Streaming allows the user to begin viewing media content before the entire file has been downloaded, by buffering a portion of the data first.

questions.text: The television signal format used in France, Russia, and a few other countries is known as:
questions.explanation: SECAM (Séquentiel couleur à mémoire) is the analog color television system first used in France and later adopted by Russia and several other nations.

questions.text: The digital video and audio compression schemes that compress a video for delivery and then decode it during playback are called:
questions.explanation: A codec (short for compressor-decompressor) is a device or computer program which encodes or decodes a digital data stream or signal.

questions.text: QuickTime movies being prepared for a CD or Web site should be processed so that the audio and video segments are interleaved together and all of the information is in one data file. This is called:
questions.explanation: In QuickTime terminology, flattening is the process of resolving all external references and interleaving data into a single, self-contained file suitable for distribution.

questions.text: The length of time it takes the CD-ROM player to locate specific data on the CD-ROM disc is called:
questions.explanation: Seek time is the delay time between the request for data and the start of data delivery from a storage device, specifically the time for the hardware to move the read head to the correct position.

questions.text: The codec that is within both AVI and QuickTime and is optimized for CD-ROM playback is:
questions.explanation: Cinepak was a very popular early video codec that was designed to play back high-quality video from CD-ROM drives which had limited transfer speeds.

questions.text: In a video camera, the sensor that picks up light is called a CCD. CCD stands for:
questions.explanation: A Charge-Coupled Device (CCD) is an integrated circuit containing an array of linked, or coupled, capacitors that convert light into an electrical charge.

questions.text: A computer's output on the monitor is calibrated to display an image:

choices.label: A
choices.text: smaller than the actual monitor's capability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: larger than the actual monitor's capability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: exactly the same size as the actual monitor's capability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: that adjusts automatically to the monitor's capability.

choices.label: E
choices.text: of a fixed size, whether it is larger or smaller than the monitor's capability depends on the monitor.

questions.explanation: In multimedia production, monitors are typically calibrated with a 'safe area' so that the displayed image is slightly smaller than the tube's full potential, ensuring that all important content is visible and not cut off by the monitor's edges.

questions.text: Removing a residual magnetic field that distorts the colors on a television screen is called:
questions.explanation: Degaussing is the process of eliminating or reducing a remnant magnetic field that can build up in CRT (Cathode Ray Tube) displays, causing color distortion.

questions.text: A video signal transmitted with all the signals mixed together and carried on a single cable is called:
questions.explanation: Composite video combines all video components—brightness (luma) and color (chroma)—into a single signal that can be carried on one wire/connector.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a television signal format?
questions.explanation: MPEG is a set of standards for digital video compression, whereas NTSC, PAL, and SECAM are specific analog television signal standards (and HDTV is a digital broadcast format).

questions.text: Computer monitors draw the lines of an entire frame in a single pass; this technique is called:
questions.explanation: Progressive scanning draws all lines of a video frame in sequence from top to bottom in a single pass, which differs from interlaced scanning used in older television systems.

questions.text: The video technique that allows you to choose a color or range of colors that become transparent, allowing the video image to be visible behind those colors in the overlying image, is known by all of the following except:

choices.label: E
choices.text: All of the above are appropriate names.

questions.explanation: Interlacing is a method of displaying video frames by alternating between even and odd lines; it has nothing to do with color transparency or keying (like Blue screen or chroma key).

questions.text: Which of the following is a videotape format?
questions.explanation: Hi-8 is a specific analog 8mm videotape format. While S-VHS is also a tape format, Hi-8 is commonly cited as the correct answer in this specific curriculum context.

questions.text: Red or green should be avoided as cue colors because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: they represent negative ideas in some cultures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: they do not blend well with other colors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: color-blind individuals cannot see them correctly.

questions.explanation: Red-green color blindness is the most common form of color vision deficiency, making those colors unreliable for communicating information or cues to a broad audience.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a good idea when creating titles (text) to be used in video?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fonts for titles should be plain, sans serif, and bold enough to be easily read.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When you are laying text onto a dark background, use white or a light color for the text.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do not kern your letters too tightly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If you use underlining or drawn graphics, make sure your lines are only one pixel wide.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Use a drop shadow to help separate the text from the background.

questions.explanation: One-pixel-wide lines are often thinner than the individual fields in an interlaced video signal, which causes the lines to flicker or disappear and reappear during playback.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a recommendation for caring for videotapes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always fast-forward new tapes to the end and then rewind them to make sure that tape tension is even from beginning to end.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Run your tape through the recorder once with the lens cap on and without audio input.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do not reuse 8 mm video cassettes, as their small size makes them susceptible to stretch.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Always make a backup copy of these tapes before you begin editing.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Never remove the break-off tab on the back of your original video cassettes; doing so renders the tape unusable with some decks.

questions.explanation: Removing the break-off tab is actually a recommended practice for protecting masters because it prevents the tape from being accidentally recorded over or erased.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a codec?
questions.explanation: NTSC is a broadcast signal standard, not a digital compression-decompression algorithm (codec).

questions.text: Generation loss occurs when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: an analog tape is copied to another analog tape.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a digital tape is copied to another digital tape.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a digital file is copied to another hard drive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a digital file is compressed with a lossy codec.

choices.label: E
choices.text: your teenage son gets his tongue pierced.

questions.explanation: Generation loss is the cumulative loss of quality between subsequent copies of data; it is a significant issue with analog media where each copy introduces noise and signal degradation.

questions.text: MPEG stands for:
questions.explanation: MPEG is the acronym for the Moving Picture Experts Group, the committee that sets international standards for digital video and audio compression.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT recommended for optimal video playback from CD-ROM?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use a large video window and a high frame rate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Interleave the audio and video segments together.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Defragment your files before burning the master.

questions.explanation: Large windows and high frame rates require high data transfer speeds. Older CD-ROM technology had limited bandwidth, so smaller windows and lower frame rates were recommended to prevent stuttering.
